section_header: 4.1. Action on Governorate buildings and services
policy_statement: Luxor will implement actions on Governorate buildings and services (street lighting, water distribution & sanitation, waste management) as the first priority within its direct responsibility to demonstrate leadership and enable wider stakeholder mobilisation.
verbatim_text: The first priority of the Governorate and the City Council should be to act on its direct perimeter of responsibility: Governorate buildings and services (street lighting, water distribution & sanitation, waste management). It is only while being exemplary on its own perimeter that the City Council will be able to promote the mobilisation of all stakeholders...
extraction_rationale: Defines an action area with multiple subsections and specific measures that follow.
mechanism_description: The Governorate and City Council will act first on their own buildings and municipal services (e.g., street lighting, water/sanitation, waste management) to reduce operational emissions and demonstrate leadership that supports wider stakeholder mobilisation.
gate_demoted_reason: evidence quote missing one of: [efficien, kwh, kw·h, per capita, ...]; evidence quote missing secondary subtype token: [efficien, kwh, kw·h, per capita, ...]
primary_causal_pathway: Municipal operations → ↓ energy/fuel use and/or ↓ carbon intensity → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in system/activity: The city/governorate upgrades and manages its own assets and service delivery (e.g., building efficiency, street-lighting retrofits, pump and treatment energy efficiency, waste operations improvements, fleet/vehicle changes, procurement and O&M practices).  
(2) Mediating physical process: Reduced electricity and fuel demand and/or substitution toward lower-carbon electricity/fuels decreases combustion- and grid-related CO₂ emissions; in some cases improved waste operations can also reduce CH₄/N₂O from municipal waste handling.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in municipal-scope GHG emissions (often Scope 1 and 2, sometimes parts of Scope 3), contributing to overall community mitigation and potentially enabling broader adoption through demonstration.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced greenhouse-gas emissions from municipal buildings and service operations via lower energy/fuel use and cleaner energy.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Actions in municipal buildings/services primarily reduce energy use or carbon intensity, leading to lower GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While operational efficiency is a form of “resource efficiency,” the dominant climate outcome across all such policies is emissions abatement (mitigation), not adaptation (hazard/exposure reduction) or nature-based carbon sequestration.  
3) Edge cases applied: Some municipal service actions could be adaptation-oriented (e.g., flood-proofing facilities) or nature-based (e.g., urban tree planting on municipal land), but those would constitute different mechanisms; this mechanism is explicitly about operational emissions reduction and leadership-by-example.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy framed as “municipal operations/actions first” to cut operational emissions shares the same primary mitigation pathway regardless of which service line (buildings, lighting, water, waste) is targeted.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether the main lever is efficiency, electrification, renewable procurement, methane control, or procurement practice depends on policy specifics and should be assigned at the individual policy level based on evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If a “municipal operations first” policy is primarily about resilience/continuity of services (e.g., hardening water/wastewater assets against climate hazards) rather than emissions, it would shift to Adaptation; similarly, if framed around ecosystem restoration on municipal land, it could be Nature-Based Solutions. In the provided description, the explicit goal is operational emissions reduction, so Mitigation is appropriate.
key_indicators: “act on its direct perimeter of responsibility”; “Governorate buildings and services”; “street lighting”; “water distribution & sanitation”; “waste management”; “first priority”; “being exemplary… to promote the mobilisation of all stakeholders”
co_benefits: Improved service reliability and quality (lighting, water/sanitation, waste); reduced local air pollution/noise (if energy/fuel use declines); potential public health and cleanliness benefits; potential municipal cost savings.
instance_edge_case_notes: The text is a high-level commitment and does not specify the exact instrument mix (e.g., retrofit program, procurement, performance standards). Classified as infrastructure-investment because it implies acting via municipal asset/service improvements under direct responsibility rather than setting rules for others.

section_header: Municipal buildings – 4.1.1.2 Energy savings / short-term actions
policy_statement: For municipal buildings, the Municipality will implement a staff awareness campaign and behavioural measures to reduce electricity use (targeting a 10% cut) and promote an internal challenge supported by the Environmental Awareness Unit (EAU).
parent_statement: Luxor will implement actions on Governorate buildings and services (street lighting, water distribution & sanitation, waste management) as the first priority within its direct responsibility to demonstrate leadership and enable wider stakeholder mobilisation.
verbatim_text:
More efforts should be done in raising staff awareness on simple change of behaviour to save energy without significant investment... 

It would be interesting to promote a challenge between services/units to invite all workers from the Governorate to make significant efforts in saving energy.

The best performing service would be celebrated as 'energy saver/climate saver'' and would get a special gratification. The Environmental Awareness Unit (EAU) within the Governorate Communication Department will promote these activities.
extraction_rationale: Concrete intervention (awareness/behaviour measures, competition) explicitly planned for municipal buildings.
mechanism_description: The municipality plans to raise staff awareness and run an internal challenge to encourage behaviour changes in municipal buildings that reduce electricity use and related emissions.
gate_demoted_reason: evidence quote missing one of: [ghg, greenhouse gas, greenhouse gases, emission, ...]; evidence quote missing secondary subtype token: [hazard, exposure, vulnerab, resilien, ...]
primary_causal_pathway: Human behavior in buildings → ↓ electricity use → ↓ fossil generation/combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Municipal staff/occupants in municipal buildings adopt energy-saving behaviors (e.g., switching off lights and IT equipment, reducing standby loads, managing HVAC setpoints and schedules, conserving during peak periods), often reinforced through campaigns and competitions.  
2) Mediating physical process: Reduced end-use electricity consumption lowers set electricity generation on the grid (and may lower peak load), decreasing fuel combustion and associated CO₂ (and other) emissions at power plants.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in GHG emissions attributable to municipal building electricity use (and sometimes ancillary reductions in waste heat and local air pollutants).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced electricity demand leading to lower grid GHG emissions (and potentially reduced peak demand).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Behavior change reduces electricity consumption in buildings, which reduces grid energy production and thus GHG emissions—this is a direct emissions-reduction pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although it can improve “resource efficiency” (using less energy for the same service), the dominant climate-relevant intent and effect is emissions mitigation via reduced energy demand; adaptation benefits (e.g., comfort during heat) are not the primary causal chain described.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a campaign primarily targets resilience behaviors (e.g., heatwave safety) it would shift toward Adaptation, but this mechanism explicitly targets electricity reduction and emissions. If paired with major retrofits, the retrofit mechanism would be separate; here the lever is behavioral/operational change.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy using this canonical mechanism (behavior-change campaigns aimed at reducing building energy use) primarily reduces energy demand and associated GHG emissions, fitting Mitigation across contexts regardless of grid mix (magnitude varies but direction is consistent).  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Any further subtype (e.g., demand-side management, public sector leadership, peak reduction) depends on policy-specific design and evidence and is deferred to policy-level assessment.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the electricity grid is already near-zero carbon, the GHG mitigation impact may be smaller and the “resource efficiency” framing could become more salient; however, the dominant intended climate pathway of this mechanism remains emissions mitigation via reduced energy demand.
key_indicators: "raising staff awareness"; "simple change of behaviour to save energy"; "promote a challenge between services/units"; "best performing service would be celebrated"; "energy saver/climate saver"; "special gratification"; "saving energy"
co_benefits: Reduced energy bills/operational costs; potential local air quality benefits from reduced power generation; improved staff engagement
instance_edge_case_notes: Includes a minor incentive element ("special gratification"), but the core instrument is an awareness/behavior campaign and internal challenge; classified under information-disclosure rather than incentive-program because no structured financial subsidy/rebate scheme is specified.
parent_policy_name: Luxor will implement actions on Governorate buildings and services (street lighting, water distribution & sanitation, waste management) as the first priority within its direct responsibility to demonstrate leadership and enable wider stakeholder mobilisation.
rescue_reasoning: Climate relevance is explicit in the source text and includes a quantified mitigation outcome. policy_statement: "behavioural measures to reduce electricity use (targeting a 10% cut)" indicates direct energy-demand reduction (mitigation). verbatim_text repeatedly targets "save energy" / "saving energy" through staff behavior change and an internal challenge. Given climate_screen = explicit_self, the row should be kept despite the token-gate failure ("no Mitigation outcome token found").

section_header: Municipal buildings – 4.1.1.3 Energy-savings / long-term actions
policy_statement: For municipal buildings, the Governorate will implement a long-term programme to replace equipment with energy-efficient systems, including switching to LED lighting and installing motion sensors, and improving heating/cooling management systems.
parent_statement: Luxor will implement actions on Governorate buildings and services (street lighting, water distribution & sanitation, waste management) as the first priority within its direct responsibility to demonstrate leadership and enable wider stakeholder mobilisation.
verbatim_text:
Beyond promoting awareness and behavioural change, it is necessary to develop a clear and comprehensive plan to replace old devices and equipment and install energy efficient systems...

- -Switch from AC/Heating reverse systems to central management of heating and cooling; and
- -Switch to LED lighting and install motion sensors in offices.
extraction_rationale: Specific technology interventions are explicitly proposed as a programme for municipal buildings.
mechanism_description: The Governorate will implement a plan to replace old equipment with energy-efficient systems (such as LED lighting, motion sensors, and improved heating/cooling management) to reduce energy use in municipal buildings.
gate_demoted_reason: evidence quote missing one of: [ghg, greenhouse gas, greenhouse gases, emission, ...]; row context missing primary/climate token: [ghg, greenhouse gas, greenhouse gases, emission, ...]
primary_causal_pathway: Same building service → ↓ energy input → ↓ fuel combustion / ↓ grid generation → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Activity/system changed: Building design and operations are upgraded via retrofits (e.g., insulation, air sealing, efficient HVAC, heat recovery, LEDs, building automation, commissioning) and improved operational practices.  
2) Mediating physical process: Reduced heat losses/gains and improved equipment efficiency/control lowers final energy demand; this in turn reduces electricity generation and on-site fuel combustion needed to provide the same services.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower CO₂ (and some CH₄/N₂O) emissions from reduced energy use, with magnitude depending on the local electricity/heat carbon intensity and baseline performance.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced GHG emissions due to lower energy consumption for the same building services (heating, cooling, lighting, plug loads).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Retrofit-driven energy efficiency reduces energy demand for the same services, which primarily reduces combustion and grid generation emissions—i.e., a direct mitigation pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While retrofits can also improve comfort, resilience (e.g., better thermal performance during heat/cold events), and reduce energy bills (resource efficiency framing), the largest climate-relevant effect across policies using this mechanism is reduced GHG emissions from lower energy consumption.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a retrofit increases electricity use (e.g., electrification with heat pumps) but reduces total emissions due to cleaner electricity, that is a different mechanism (fuel switching/electrification). If retrofits are primarily for passive survivability/adaptation without energy/emissions objectives, that would shift to Adaptation, but that is not what this mechanism encodes.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy that fits “commercial/municipal building retrofit → energy use reduction” shares the same primary effect—lower energy demand leading to lower associated emissions—regardless of city or building type.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether the policy should be tagged secondarily as Resource Efficiency (cost/energy savings emphasis) or Adaptation co-benefits (thermal resilience) depends on policy-level evidence and is not assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some retrofit packages include fuel switching (e.g., gas boiler to heat pump) or on-site renewables; those would introduce additional/alternative mechanisms. Rebound effects (increased comfort leading to higher use) can reduce net savings but typically do not overturn the dominant mitigation pathway.
key_indicators: “develop a clear and comprehensive plan to replace old devices and equipment”; “install energy efficient systems”; “Switch…to central management of heating and cooling”; “Switch to LED lighting and install motion sensors”
co_benefits: Reduced operating costs; improved indoor comfort; reduced local air pollutants from lower energy consumption
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a procurement requirement (buy efficient equipment), but the emphasis is on a long-term retrofit programme/plan to replace and install systems across municipal buildings, aligning more with an investment program.
parent_policy_name: Luxor will implement actions on Governorate buildings and services (street lighting, water distribution & sanitation, waste management) as the first priority within its direct responsibility to demonstrate leadership and enable wider stakeholder mobilisation.
rescue_reasoning: Climate relevance is explicit in the measure content (energy efficiency retrofit in buildings). Policy_statement commits to "replace equipment with energy-efficient systems, including switching to LED lighting and installing motion sensors" and "improving heating/cooling management systems." Verbatim_text repeats: "replace old devices and equipment and install energy efficient systems" plus "Switch from AC/Heating ... to central management" and "Switch to LED lighting and install motion sensors." These actions reduce building energy consumption, aligning with Mitigation (energy_use_reduction), even though no explicit emissions/outcome token is present. With climate_screen=explicit_self, retain the row; the gate failure is due to overly strict token requirements, not lack of climate content.

section_header: Street lighting – 4.1.2.2 Revolving fund to replace old lamps
policy_statement: The City of Luxor will create a revolving fund to replace old street lighting lamps with LED, reinvesting savings to achieve full replacement over time.
parent_statement: Luxor will implement actions on Governorate buildings and services (street lighting, water distribution & sanitation, waste management) as the first priority within its direct responsibility to demonstrate leadership and enable wider stakeholder mobilisation.
verbatim_text: The City of Luxor looks for an initial financial support to feed in a revolving fund dedicated to old lamps replacement. The City should set up a specific budget monitoring mechanism and put aside financial resources preserved due to more efficient lamps replacing old devices. Resources saved will be invested again in lamp replacement.
extraction_rationale: Defines a financing mechanism (revolving fund) and reinvestment approach for LED street lighting replacement.
mechanism_description: The city plans to use a revolving fund to replace old street lighting lamps with more efficient LEDs, reducing electricity consumption and reinvesting the resulting savings to fund additional replacements over time.
gate_demoted_reason: evidence quote missing one of: [efficien, kwh, kw·h, per capita, ...]; evidence quote missing secondary subtype token: [ghg, greenhouse gas, greenhouse gases, emission, ...]
primary_causal_pathway: Same service (street lighting) → ↓ electricity input → ↓ fuel combustion for power → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Municipal street-lighting infrastructure is upgraded from older, less efficient lamps/ballasts to LEDs (often with improved drivers/controls) while maintaining comparable illumination levels.  
2) Mediating physical process: LEDs provide more lumens per watt and better optical control, reducing electrical power set and total kWh consumed over operating hours.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced electricity demand lowers marginal electricity generation and associated CO₂e emissions (with magnitude depending on grid emissions factor), yielding mitigation benefits via efficiency.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced grid electricity consumption for public lighting, leading to avoided power-sector GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Efficiency retrofit delivers the same lighting service with less electricity, which reduces upstream generation and GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although the objective is ultimately emissions reduction, the direct mechanism is reduced energy input per service (efficiency), which aligns best with Resource Efficiency rather than a direct fuel-switching Mitigation pathway.  
3) Edge cases applied: If electricity is already near-zero-carbon, emissions benefits shrink but the mechanism remains energy-efficiency; if the retrofit includes major smart controls, it still primarily acts through electricity reduction.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any “street lighting LED retrofit → electricity use reduction” policy primarily operates by lowering kWh for lighting, irrespective of financing method (e.g., revolving fund, ESCO).  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any further sub-classification (e.g., municipal operations, public lighting controls, financing type) requires policy-specific evidence and is not set at the mechanism level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the local grid is fully renewable/nuclear, the mitigation impact (avoided CO₂e) may be small, but the primary mechanism remains energy/resource efficiency; rebound effects are minimal because lighting levels are typically regulated, though “over-lighting” could partially offset savings in some implementations.
key_indicators: "revolving fund"; "initial financial support"; "dedicated to old lamps replacement"; "budget monitoring mechanism"; "put aside financial resources preserved due to more efficient lamps"; "Resources saved will be invested again in lamp replacement"
co_benefits: Improved lighting quality and safety; reduced municipal operating costs/budget relief; potential reduced maintenance due to longer lamp life
instance_edge_case_notes: Although it results in an equipment upgrade (infrastructure change), the policy lever described is the financing/reinvestment mechanism; classification therefore favors an incentive/financing program over infrastructure-investment as the instrument type.
parent_policy_name: Luxor will implement actions on Governorate buildings and services (street lighting, water distribution & sanitation, waste management) as the first priority within its direct responsibility to demonstrate leadership and enable wider stakeholder mobilisation.

section_header: Street lighting – 4.1.2.2 Street lighting strategic plan
policy_statement: The City of Luxor will develop a street lighting strategic plan with differentiated lighting levels and technical solutions (e.g., sensors, midnight reduction) supported by public consultation and monitoring of energy and budget savings.
parent_statement: Luxor will implement actions on Governorate buildings and services (street lighting, water distribution & sanitation, waste management) as the first priority within its direct responsibility to demonstrate leadership and enable wider stakeholder mobilisation.
verbatim_text:
The City of Luxor would gain from developing a street lighting strategic plan, identifying areas of differentiated usage... 

Such an improvement... should lead to designing a lighting system combining qualitative lighting and reduced energy consumption. ...

1. Based on the city development plan identify 3 or 4 types of streets and areas...
2. Organise public consultation...
... 4. Implement step by step the plan... Such monitoring must measure energy consumption reduction and highlight what it means in budget cuts.
extraction_rationale: Concrete planning instrument with implementation steps and monitoring requirements.
mechanism_description: The city plans to implement a street-lighting strategy (e.g., differentiated lighting levels, sensors, and nighttime reduction) and monitor results to reduce electricity use and the emissions associated with that power use.
gate_demoted_reason: evidence_quote not substring-grounded in source
primary_causal_pathway: Same lighting service → ↓ electricity input → ↓ GHG emissions from power generation → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: The city adjusts how public street lighting is provided—e.g., dimming at low-traffic hours, differentiated illumination levels, motion/ambient-light sensors, improved controls/monitoring—to deliver set lighting with less run-time and/or lower wattage. (2) Process: Reduced electrical load and operating hours decrease kilowatt-hours consumed by the lighting system, reducing demand on the grid (or local generators). (3) Outcome: Lower electricity generation leads to lower CO₂e emissions (and typically cost savings) attributable to street lighting energy use.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced grid electricity demand from street lighting, leading to lower associated GHG emissions from power generation.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: optimize lighting operations/controls → reduce electricity used for the same service → reduce upstream GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: The mechanism’s core lever is lowering energy input (efficiency/optimization). Mitigation is an outcome (lower emissions) but achieved via resource/energy efficiency rather than primarily via decarbonizing supply.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a program is primarily a fuel switch to renewable electricity or onsite solar for lighting, that would shift toward Mitigation (clean energy); if framed mainly for public safety/resilience during outages, that could introduce Adaptation, but that is not the dominant pathway here.  
4) Confirm applies to all instances: Any “street-lighting optimization” policy (dimming, sensors, scheduling, differentiated levels, control systems) has the same primary effect of reducing electricity consumption for lighting, so Resource Efficiency is consistent across instances.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any further categorization (e.g., digital controls vs LED retrofits vs operational curfew) requires policy-specific evidence and is deferred to the policy level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the policy bundles optimization with large-scale LED replacement, it remains Resource Efficiency; if it is mainly powered by renewable generation procurement/installation, primary could shift to Mitigation. If lighting reductions materially affect safety, equity, or service levels, “same service” may not hold, but climate pathway classification is unchanged.
key_indicators: "developing a street lighting strategic plan"; "differentiated lighting levels"; "technical solutions (e.g., sensors, midnight reduction)"; "reduced energy consumption"; "monitoring must measure energy consumption reduction"; "highlight what it means in budget cuts"
co_benefits: Improved night-time safety/visibility and potential reduction in light pollution
instance_edge_case_notes: Although it mentions “Implement step by step the plan,” the core policy action described is plan development plus consultation/monitoring (not a binding standard or funded retrofit), so it is classified as a planning/strategy instrument with enabling directness.
parent_policy_name: Luxor will implement actions on Governorate buildings and services (street lighting, water distribution & sanitation, waste management) as the first priority within its direct responsibility to demonstrate leadership and enable wider stakeholder mobilisation.

section_header: Water delivery – 4.1.3.2 Action to promote
policy_statement: For water delivery, Governorate services will conduct a detailed assessment of the water distribution network and wastewater treatment to identify energy-saving investments (e.g., variable speed pumps, high-efficiency equipment) and consider establishing a SCADA monitoring and control system.
parent_statement: Luxor will implement actions on Governorate buildings and services (street lighting, water distribution & sanitation, waste management) as the first priority within its direct responsibility to demonstrate leadership and enable wider stakeholder mobilisation.
verbatim_text: The Governorate services should conduct a detailed assessment of the current status of the water distribution network and the wastewater treatment process. ... switching from classical pumps to variable speed devices and high efficiency equipment might result in cutting electricity consumption by 30% or more. Likewise establishing a SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition) system for monitoring and control could help...
extraction_rationale: Specific interventions and assessment commitment for reducing water-sector electricity use.
mechanism_description: Governorate services plan to assess water distribution and wastewater treatment operations and pursue efficiency upgrades (e.g., variable speed pumps, high-efficiency equipment) and possibly SCADA monitoring/control to cut electricity consumption.
gate_demoted_reason: evidence quote missing one of: [ghg, greenhouse gas, greenhouse gases, emission, ...]; evidence quote missing secondary subtype token: [hazard, exposure, vulnerab, resilien, ...]
primary_causal_pathway: Same water/wastewater service → ↓ energy input (electricity/fuels) → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Utilities/governorate operators retrofit and optimize pumps, aeration, motors, and process equipment (e.g., variable speed drives, high-efficiency units) and add monitoring/control (e.g., SCADA) to run systems closer to optimal setpoints and schedules.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Improved electromechanical and process efficiency reduces kWh set per m³ delivered/treated by lowering friction/overpumping, improving pump curve matching, reducing unnecessary head/pressure, and optimizing treatment energy (e.g., aeration control).  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower energy demand reduces combustion and grid-generation emissions (CO₂e); in some cases also reduces peak demand and upstream fuel use.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced electricity use in water and wastewater operations, leading to lower GHG emissions from energy supply.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: efficiency upgrades in water/wastewater operations reduce energy consumption, which reduces GHG emissions—this is a mitigation pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although water-sector measures can support adaptation (service continuity under heat/drought) or resource efficiency (water loss reduction), the mechanism as stated is explicitly energy-efficiency/energy-use reduction as the main lever, making emissions reduction the dominant climate effect.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a specific policy instance focuses mainly on leak reduction or demand reduction (water saved) with energy savings as secondary, it could trend toward Resource Efficiency; if focused on resilience/backup power, it could trend toward Adaptation. Those are not the defining features of this mechanism.  
4) Confirm primary applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that upgrades water/wastewater equipment/controls to cut electricity use will, in general, primarily reduce energy-related emissions, regardless of location or grid mix (magnitude varies, direction consistent).  
5) Note on secondary subtype: Any secondary categorization (e.g., also reducing non-revenue water, improving reliability, enabling better methane management) must be assigned at the individual policy level based on explicit evidence, and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If upgrades increase treatment intensity (e.g., higher-pressure operation or added treatment stages) energy use could rise despite efficiency measures; conversely, SCADA may primarily be installed for reliability/leak management rather than energy. Classification should be revisited only if a policy’s stated primary intent/effect is not energy reduction.
key_indicators: “conduct a detailed assessment”; “identify energy-saving investments”; “variable speed devices”; “high efficiency equipment”; “cutting electricity consumption by 30% or more”; “establishing a SCADA … system for monitoring and control”
co_benefits: Improved service reliability/continuity, reduced operating costs, better leak/pressure management, improved wastewater treatment performance and compliance.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be coded as infrastructure-investment if the policy were framed as committing funds/implementation for pumps/SCADA; here it is primarily an assessment to identify/consider investments, so planning-strategy with enabling directness fits best.
parent_policy_name: Luxor will implement actions on Governorate buildings and services (street lighting, water distribution & sanitation, waste management) as the first priority within its direct responsibility to demonstrate leadership and enable wider stakeholder mobilisation.
rescue_reasoning: Climate screen is explicit_self.
